svg.title: Audio streaming methods comparison
svg.desc: A diagram comparing five audio streaming methods from backend to frontend, with latency and use case labels

g.onclick: sendPrompt('Tell me more about progressive download audio streaming')
g.text: 1. Progressive download (HTTP range requests)
Client fetches file in chunks via HTTP Range. Simple, no special server needed.
Not real streaming — must buffer large chunks before playback.
High latency

g.onclick: sendPrompt('Explain HLS and DASH adaptive bitrate streaming')
g.text: 2. HLS / DASH (adaptive bitrate streaming)
Audio split into 2–10s segments served over HTTP. Client picks quality based on bandwidth.
Used by Spotify (OGG chunks), YouTube Music, Apple Music. CDN-friendly.
Startup latency: 2–10 s. Seek latency: 0.5–2 s.
Medium latency

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How does HTTP chunked transfer encoding work for audio streaming?')
g.text: 3. HTTP chunked transfer (streaming response)
Server streams audio bytes in chunks using Transfer-Encoding: chunked.
Backend pushes data as it's ready. Good for AI-generated or live audio.
Startup latency: 0.3–1 s. Works with fetch() + ReadableStream on the client.
Low latency

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How to stream audio over WebSockets in a music app?')
g.text: 4. WebSocket binary streaming
Full-duplex persistent connection. Server pushes raw PCM or Opus frames in real time.
Best for real-time collaboration, live radio, AI voice. Uses Web Audio API on client.
Startup latency: <100 ms. Ideal for interactive or bidirectional audio.
Very low latency

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How does WebRTC work for audio streaming?')
g.text: 5. WebRTC (peer-to-peer / SFU)
SRTP over UDP. Jitter buffers + congestion control built-in. P2P or via SFU.
Used for live concerts, voice calls, watch-parties. Opus codec standard.
Startup latency: 20–80 ms. Glass-to-glass sub-100ms possible.
Ultra-low latency

svg.text: On-demand music → HLS/DASH with short segments (2s) + CDN edge caching.
Podcasts / radio → Chunked HTTP or WebSocket for progressive playback.
Live / social audio → WebRTC for real-time with SFU (e.g. LiveKit, mediasoup).
